coredata.dc:title: Novel CdS quantum dots templated hydrogel nanocomposites: Synthesis, characterization, swelling and dye adsorption properties
coredata.dc:creator: Hosseinzadeh, Hossein
Bahador, Negar
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogel nanocomposites (HNCs) of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and poly(acrylic acid-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid) were prepared via in situ cross-linking copolymerization method followed by incorporation of cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS-QDs) within the network. The structure and surface morphology of the prepared HNCs were fully characterized using FTIR, SEM, EDX, XRD, TGA/DTG, UV–Vis, TEM and DLS techniques along with fluorescent microscopy. The influence of Na2S, CdCl2, and monomers concentrations onto swelling capacity of HNCs were optimized and a maximum swelling of 154g/g was determined. Moreover, the HNC was employed for the adsorption of cationic dye, Rhodamine B (RhB), from aqueous solution. The impacts of experimental variables on the removal of RhB were explored. The adsorption process was followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the experimental data also fitted well with the Langmuir isotherm model with a maximum RhB adsorption capacity of 137mg/g. Conclusively, the HNCs could be used as an efficient adsorbent for the removal of RhB dye from contaminated water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Nanocomposite
Dye adsorption
Quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dye desorption and regeneration of HNC
Effect of parameters on RhB adsorption capacity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Digital images of the produced hydrogel nanocomposite before and after swelling in water.
Infrared spectra of CMC, hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbent.
The SEM-EDS spectra of CMC, hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent.
The TEM images of CMC, hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent and the related histogram of HNC adsorbent.
XRD patterns of pure CMC and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent.
TGA thermograms of CMC, hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbent.
Fluorescence emission spectra and fluorescent microscopic images of hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbent at 25°C.
Effect of concentrations of AMPS, AA, cadmium and sulfide on swelling capacity and swelling kinetics of HNC adsorbent in water.
Effect of adsorbent dose (A), initial dye concentration (A), dye solution pH (B), [CdCl2]/[Na2S] ratio (C) and ionic strength (D) on dye adsorption capacity of HNC adsorbent.
Comparison of the adsorption equilibrium isotherms of RhB dye onto HNC with the experimental points.
The reusability of the hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbents at four different temperatures.
Schematic illustration of the HNC formation.
A schematic representation of in situ formation of CdS particles in the hydrogel nanocomposite network.
Schematic representation of main electrostatic interactions between negative and positive functional groups of HNC adsorbent and RhB dye adsorbate.
The chemical structure and some properties of RhB.
Kinetic parameters for the adsorption of RhB onto the hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC).
Parameters of the Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, and Redlich-Peterson isotherm models for adsorption of MB onto the hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbent.

head.ce:title: Novel CdS quantum dots templated hydrogel nanocomposites: Synthesis, characterization, swelling and dye adsorption properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel nanocomposites (HNCs) of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and poly(acrylic acid-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid) were prepared via in situ cross-linking copolymerization method followed by incorporation of cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS-QDs) within the network. The structure and surface morphology of the prepared HNCs were fully characterized using FTIR, SEM, EDX, XRD, TGA/DTG, UV–Vis, TEM and DLS techniques along with fluorescent microscopy.

The influence of Na2S, CdCl2, and monomers concentrations onto swelling capacity of HNCs were optimized and a maximum swelling of 154g/g was determined. Moreover, the HNC was employed for the adsorption of cationic dye, Rhodamine B (RhB), from aqueous solution. The impacts of experimental variables on the removal of RhB were explored. The adsorption process was followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the experimental data also fitted well with the Langmuir isotherm model with a maximum RhB adsorption capacity of 137mg/g. Conclusively, the HNCs could be used as an efficient adsorbent for the removal of RhB dye from contaminated water.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer nanocomposites including fluorescent dots (QDs) have been synthesized for various fields such as catalyst, solar cells, drug delivery, optical sensing, bioimaging and optoelectronics [1–8]. QDs (also known as semiconductors nano-particles) having sizes below 10nm have been studied as valuable semiconductor metal nanoparticles [9] with unique optical and electronic properties [10]. Among QDs, cadmium sulfide (CdS) has drawn increasing attention due to their easy availability, cost-effectiveness, thermal stability, low cytotoxicity and sufficient band gap energy (2.4eV) at room temperature [11–13]. However, QDs systems in defined geometrices getting controllable properties are very important in terms of their sizes, shapes, morphologies, and physical and chemical characteristics. So, the bare QDs usually modified with various ligands using various methods to synthesis QDs with different properties [14–16]. Polymers with various functional groups generally were employed in nanoparticles preparation [17–19].

In this regard, hydrogels as three-dimensional polymeric networks with the ability to absorb large quantities of water can provide unique environments for synthesis of QDs [20]. They have used in many applications such as wound dressings [21], contact lenses [22], artificial organs [23], tissue engineering [24] and drug delivery systems [25] where water absorbance or water retention is important [26]. The higher production cost and low gel strength of these polymers, however, restrict their application widely. To improve these limitations, inorganic compounds with low cost can be used for synthesis of hydrogel nanocomposites. In recent years, a few research works were reported in the case of QDs templated hydrogels, since its first report by Sun et al. [27]. Sahiner et al. demonstrated the preparation of hydrogel templated CdS-QDs based on AMPS [28]. Konwar et al. showed a simple method for the synthesis of carbon dots from a new green source “tea” [29]. Rezanejhad et al. reported preparation of starch-capped CdS-QDs [30]. Other methods of preparing of QDs templated polymeric network from egg white [31], ascorbic acid [32], orange juice [33], bovine serum albumin [34], peach gum polysaccharide [35], coffee grounds [36] and hydrothermal carbonization of glucose [37] and chitosan [38] carbohydrates has also been reported recently.

In this work, we reported a facile method for synthesis of CdS-QDs incorporated within hydrophilic carboxymethyl cellulose-graft-poly(acrylic acid-co-2-acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid) hydrogel network. The structure, swelling behavior and dye adsorption property of the as-prepared hydrogel nanocomposites were studied in detail.

The polysaccharide, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany), N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA, from Merck), ammonium persulfate (APS, from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Loius, MO, USA), sodium sulfide (from Merck), and calcium chloride (from Merck) were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Acrylic acid (AA, from Merck) was used after vacuum distillation. 2-Acrylamido-2-methylpropane sulfonic acid (AMPS, from Sigma-Aldrich) was used after crystallization in acetone. The Rhodamine B (RhB) dye was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The molecular structure and identification information of RhB are depicted in Table 1 . All other chemicals were commercially available and used directly without further purification. All solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water.

Scheme 1 shows a simple illustration of in situ synthesis of CdS-QDs by simultaneous formation of superabsorbent network and CdS quantum dots according to the following procedure. Firstly, certain amounts of CMC (2.0g), AA (2.0mL) and AMPS (2.0g) were added to a three-neck reactor equipped with a mechanical stirrer (RZR 2021, a three-blade propeller type, Heidolph, Schwabach, Germany), while stirring (300rpm) for 30min. The reactor was placed in a water bath preset at 70°C. After dispersing and homogenizing the mixture of reactants, certain amounts of the initiator APS (0.1g dissolved in 5mL H2O) and cross-linker MBA (0.1g dissolved in 5mL H2O) were added to the reaction mixture. The obtained hydrogel was then poured into ethanol (200mL). After complete dewatering for 24h, the hardened gel particles were filtered, washed with fresh ethanol (2×50mL) and dried at 50°C. After grinding, the powdered hydrogel was stored away from moisture, heat and light. Then, appropriate amounts of CdCl2 (100mL of 250mgL−1) and Na2S (100mL of 0.05molL−1) were added into the solution of the resulted hydrogel (0.1g of dried hydrogel dispersed in 30mL of H2O). Finally, the synthesized yellow HNC washed with ultrapure deionized water three times and dried at 70°C for 10h in vacuum oven.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of KBr pelleted (Aldrich, 99%, FT-IR grade) samples was taken using an ABB Bomem MB-100 FTIR spectrophotometer (Quebec, Canada), at room temperature with an average of 64 scans at 4cm−1 resolution. The fractured morphology of the gel was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Dried hydrogel nanocomposite powder were coated with 3nm of thin layer of palladium gold alloy and imaged in a SEM (Leo, 1455 VP) at 30kV acceleration voltages for 2min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were recorded with a Philips CM10 (UK) operating at 60kV tension. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were also recorded using a Siemens D-500 X-ray diffractometer with wavelength λ=1.54A° (Cu–Kα), at a tube voltage of 35kV, and tube current of 30mA. Thermo gravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on a Universal V4.1D TA Instruments (SDT Q600) with 8–10mg samples on a platinum pan under nitrogen atmosphere. Experiments were performed at a heating rate of 10°C/min from 25 until 600°C. Fluorescence spectrum measurements were performed by using a Varian Cary-Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The HNC samples in cylindrical form with a radius of about 5mm were cut into 10mm and 1mm slices for absorption and fluorescence measurements.

An accurately weighed sample of the powdered HNC (0.1g±0.001) with average particle sizes between 250 and 350μm was immersed in distilled water (200mL) and allowed to soak for 3h at room temperature. The equilibrium swelling (ES) capacity was measured twice at room temperature according to a conventional tea bag method by using the following formula:
(1)
ES
g / g
=
W 2
−
W 1
W 1

where W
1 and W
2 are the weights of dry and swollen hydrogel nanocomposite, respectively. Fig. 1 shows the digital images of the produced hydrogel nanocomposite before and after swelling in water.

All dye adsorption experiments were examined through a batch method on a shaker with a constant shaking speed of 120rpm and at ambient temperature (25°C). An accurately weighted of powdered sample of NHA (0.05±0.0001g) was immersed into 50mL of dye solution. The pH of the dye solution was adjusted to the desired value by the addition of 1.0molL−1 of HCl or 1.0molL−1 of NaOH solutions (prior to the addition of the adsorbent). The solution pH before and after dye adsorption was recorded with a pH meter (Metrohm/620, accuracy±0.1). Then the mixture was stirred for 24h and allowed to reach equilibrium. The HNC was then filtered off and the residual dye concentration in the solution was determined using a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV/VIS spectrophotometer at 559nm. The adsorption capacity (q
e , mg/g) was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
q e
=
C o
−
C e
× V
W

where C
o and C
e are the initial and equilibrium dye concentrations (mgL−1), respectively. V is the solution volume (L) and W is the dry weight of HNC adsorbent (g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye desorption and regeneration of HNC
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the regeneration of the hydrogel nanocomposites, dye-adsorbed nanocomposites were immersed into 40mL of 0.5molL−1 HNO3 solution at four different temperature values. Then, the suspension was stirred for 12h. Thereafter, the adsorbent was collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and dried. The adsorbent was reused for adsorption again. This adsorption-desorption procedure was repeated five times, each times with fresh solution under the same conditions.

Scheme 2 shows a simple and general schematic illustration of the preparation of HNC adsorbent by in situ CdS formation and radical copolymerization reaction. In the first step, the thermally dissociating initiator, i.e. ammonium persulfate, is decomposed under heating (70°C) to produce sulfate anion-radicals. The produced radical abstracts hydrogen from the hydroxyl groups of the CMC to form alkoxy radicals. So, this redox system initiates radically graft copolymerization of AA and AMPS monomers [39]. Since a crosslinking agent, e.g. MBA, is presented in the system, the graft copolymer comprises a crosslinked structure. Following the treatments of absorbed Cd(II) ions with Na2S solution, CdS Q-dots were prepared in situ within hydrogel [28]. As clearly seen from Scheme 2, ionic cross-linking and electrostatic interactions were carried out between main functional groups of CMC (OH and COOH), AA (COOH) and AMPS (NH and SO3H) components of the synthesized HNC and Cd2+ and also the resulted CdS-QDs nanoparticles.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), CMC-graft-poly(AA-co-AMPS) hydrogel and CMC-graft-poly(AA-co-AMPS)/CdS-QDs hydrogel nanocomposite adsorbent (HNC). In the spectrum of CMC, the peaks observed at 1059, 1427, 1610, 2916 and 3449cm−1 can be attributed to the COC stretching vibration of the glucisidic units, symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of COOH and stretching vibration of Csp3 H and OH bands of CMC polysaccharide, respectively [40]. The FTIR spectrum obtained for CMC polysaccharide was analogous to the spectrum reported previously [41–43]. The normal hydrogel (free CdS-QDs) shows peaks at 619cm−1 (SO stretching vibration), 1167cm−1 (symmetric stretching of SO), 1224cm−1 (asymmetric stretching of SO) and 1393cm−1 (bending vibration of CH3) related to AMPS monomer grafted onto CMC backbones. Moreover, the peaks at 1464, 2982 and 3435cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of conjugated CO, Csp3 H and NH bands of AMPS monomer, respectively. The wavenumbers at 1036, 1456, 1561 and 1736cm−1 are also assigned to the CO and symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of COO− and COOH grafted AA monomer, respectively [44].

In the FTIR spectra of hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent, the intensity of some peaks were reduced and also position of some bands were shifted due to the interactions of Cd (II) ions and sulfide with sulfate and carboxylate functional groups of grafted monomers onto CMC backbones. For instance, the intensity of the peak at 1736cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of hydrogel was reduced and also overlapped with a broad band in the FTIR spectrum of HNC adsorbent.

Scanning electron microscopy can be used as a valuable technique for investigation of the particle shape, porosity, and appropriate size distribution of an adsorbent. The SEM-energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) analysis of CMC, normal hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent were shown in Fig. 3 . Similar to the previously reported hydrogel polymers [45–47], porous structures can be observed from the SEM images of the prepared hydrogel and HNC samples. The SEM images of pure hydrogel and HNC adsorbent show distinct differences. Noticeably, the normal hydrogel surface was compact, flat stretch, rough as distinctive from the smooth surface of the HNC adsorbent. Moreover, compared with hydrogel without CdS-QDs, nanoparticles are found in HNC product, indicating strong interactions between CdS-QDs nanoparticles and functional groups of CMC and poly(AA-co-AMPS) components of the HNCs (see Scheme 2). The presence of pores in the form of black holes in the SEM image of HNC may be produced due to release of gases during the preparation process. Furthermore, it should be pointed out that the HNC has a large surface area, and therefore there is a good possibility of fast swelling and dyes being absorbed into these pores and surfaces [48,49].

Corresponding to SEM images, the EDS analysis of the prepared adsorbent (HNC) displays main signals for CdS-QDs, which confirm the formation of CdS-QDs nanoparticles in the as-prepared adsorbent as former proved by SEM images. In HNC sample, the occurrence of elements Cd with 9.3wt% and S with 7.9wt% corresponds to the prepared CdS-QDs nanoparticles within nanocomposite structure. Other peaks in this figure correspond to carbon and oxygen which are due to the presence of various functional groups of the CMC-graft-poly(AA-co-AMPS) of nanocomposite.

To deeply study of the adsorbent microstructure, the samples were characterized by TEM. Fig. 4 shows the TEM photographs of CMC, hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent. As shown in Fig. 4, results revealed that the polysaccharide and normal hydrogel had rough surface, which previously shown in SEM images of them. In contrast with both CMC and normal hydrogel samples, it is obvious that the CdS-QDs nanoparticles in the HNC structure are in nanometer grade and the nanocomposites are dispersed in the polymer matrix. However, some of the spherical CdS-QDs aggregated to form clusters due to interactions between functional groups of CMC-g-poly(AA-co-AMPS) and CdS-QDs nanoparticles [50] and high surface energy of their very small size [51]. The related histogram also reveals that most of the CdS-QDs nanoparticles are dispersed with diameter in the range of 5–15nm within the HNC network.

XRD analysis describes the physical properties in terms of crystalline structure [52].The peak position and the interlayer spacing are two parts of the information provided by XRD measurements. The crystalline structure of the HNC adsorbent was investigated with XRD measurement. In Fig. 5 , XRD pattern of the nanocomposite was compared with the pristine CMC. Generally, CMC showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ=18° from the intermolecular spacing of CMC [53]. This peak depicts that CMC substrate is mostly amorphous. It has been reported that CMC has a crystalinity of about 8% [54]. The very sharp and clear diffraction signals at 2θ=27, 31, 33, 41, 49 and 53° in the XRD pattern of the HNC were assigned to hexagonal wurtzite structure of CdS-QDs nanoparticles [55] incorporated in the nanocomposite network. These peaks were related to scattering from the (100), (002), (101), (220), (103) and (222) planes, respectively, which was compared with the standard JCPDS data card no: 80-1049 [56]. Thus, this result further demonstrates CdS-QDs nanoparticles were successfully fabricated in this work, which match well with those reported in the literature [57–60]. By applying Scherrer's equation [61] and by calculating the full width at half maximum of the selected XRD peak at 2θ=33°, the average diameter size of particles is also found to be in the range of 10–20nm for CdS nanoparticles.

We also determined the thermal stability of hydrogel nanocomposites by the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in comparison with the intact CMC and hydrogel without CdS-QDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , the thermal stability of the hydrogel nanocomposite was improved. This behavior can be attributed to the fact that CdS-QDs nanoparticles act as thermal and heat protective barriers in the nanocomposites and therefore the initial decomposition temperatures were increased due to the synergistic effect of CdS-QDs, CMC and grafted copolymeric chains. Generally, the incorporation of nanoparticles into the polymer matrix was found to enhance the thermal stability by acting as a superior insulator and mass transport barrier [62]. Conclusively, the HNC had a lower weight loss than intact CMC and normal hydrogel. This means that the grafting of CMC and incorporating of CdS-QDs nanoparticles increase the thermal stability of CMC to some extent. However, the weight losses in the TGA curves of all samples are due to the fragmentation and oxidative degradation of polymer backbones [63]. We also estimated the amount of CdS templated in HNC by TGA via the difference between weight loss of hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite at 600°C, similar to the method reported earlier by Sahiner et al. [28]. As shown in Fig. 6, it can be concluded that the CdS-QDs nanoparticles content of HNC was about 10% by weight.

Fig. 7 shows the UV absorption spectra of the hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite product. As clearly seen from figure, in comparison with the pure hydrogel without CdS-QDs, the HNC shows an absorption peak in 430nm which mainly is due to the unique surface Plasmon resonance absorption and scattering of light by CdS-QDs nanoparticles [64], which is in close agreement with previous works [1,30,35,65]. It has been reported that the characteristic band formation for nanosized CdS is normally observed between 400 and 500nm emission wavelengths [66]. Furthermore, the absorption band for both pure hydrogel and the HNC at 200–220nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of CO of the CMC-graft-poly(AA-co-AMPS) counterpart of the samples, thus confirming the formation of graft copolymer.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was also used for further confirmation of the presence of CdS-QDs in the hydrogel nanocomposite network structure. In fact, as a typical semiconductor, CdS-QDs exhibit interesting optical properties. As indicated in the room temperature fluorescence spectrum of HNC (Fig. 8 ), a maximum excitation wavelength observed at 470nm correspond to the CdS-QDs, which is in agreement with other similar reported works [14,30,67,68]. This emission observed at 470nm is assigned to electron-hole recombinations at surface traps [69]. Moreover, the fluorescent microscopic images of HNC were shown in Fig. 8. These images were indicated that new classes of fluorescence molecules were prepared within the HNC structure. The presence of CdS-QDs in the HNC network causes not only the protection of them against various non-suitable environments but also causes of formation of hydrogel nanocomposites with superior photoelectronic properties.

The effect of monomers concentration on the swelling capacity of the HNC was studied by varying the AA and AMPS concentration (Fig. 9 ). Enhanced monomer concentration increases the diffusion of monomers molecules into the hydrogel network and therefore causes an increase in water absorbency. In addition, higher AMPS content enhanced the hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite, which caused a stronger affinity for more absorption of water [70]. However, the swelling decrease after a certain value of AA (0.52molL−1) may be attributed to the increase in viscosity of the medium which hinders the movement of free radicals and monomer molecules [71].

Fig. 9 also shows the effect of Cd2+ and S2− concentrations onto equilibrium swelling capacity of hydrogel nanocomposites. The results indicated that water absorbency capacity was initially increased with increasing ions concentration and then decreased. The initial increment in swelling capacity values was due to higher crosslinking of polymeric chains [72]. Moreover, once the ions were introduced inside the polymer networks, the overall porosity were increased, leading to the enhancement of the swelling capacities [73]. However, swelling capacity is decreased with a further increase in the amount of cadmium and sulfide ions. When the cross-linkage points between the polymeric chains are further increased, the swelling capability of the hydrogels will be reduced. Such a behavior is well-known as reported by pioneering scientists [74,75].

The swelling values versus swelling time (swelling kinetics) of the resulted HNCs were also studied in distilled water (Fig. 9). According to the figure, the rate of water absorbency as well as the swelling capacity of the hydrogel nanocomposite is higher than those of for pure hydrogel due to the higher surface area of CdS-QDs nanoparticles within the structure of HNC. It is well known that the swelling kinetics for the adsorbent polymers is significantly influenced by particle size of them [76].

Dye removal from dye-contaminated wastewaters of various industries is a research area of increasing interest due to environmental importance [77–79]. So, in the present work, cationic dye Rhodamine B (RhB) was removed from aqueous solutions via attractive electrostatic interactions with anionic sulfunyl and carboxylate groups of hydrogel nanocomposite (HNC) adsorbent as simply shown in Scheme 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of parameters on RhB adsorption capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the effect of various main parameters on the dye adsorption capacity of HNCs. The effect of both adsorbent dose and initial dye concentration can be seen from Fig. 10A. First, it is observed that the adsorption of RhB dye onto HNC adsorbent increased with HNC dose. It is obvious that increase in adsorbent dose provides better opportunity for interaction between the adsorbent and the RhB dye molecules. Second, it was clear from Fig. 10A that the dye adsorption capacity increased initially with an increase in the RhB concentration. This is attributed to the greater availability of dye molecules in the vicinity of the HNC adsorbents.

The effect of pH as an important parameter on the adsorption capacity was also studied (Fig. 10B). Results indicated that an increase in dye solution pH from 2 to 10 increases RhB adsorption capacity of HNC adsorbent. This behavior is due to the fact that at basic solutions with higher pHs the strong electrostatic interactions occurred between the negatively charged groups of HNC and positively charged groups of RhB dye molecules, which caused enhancement of the adsorption capacity.

The effect of CdCl2 and Na2S concentrations onto the absorption of RhB dye was also investigated (Fig. 10C). As shown, the dye adsorption capacity of the adsorbent increased with increase in cadmium and sulfide ions content. As mentioned previously, this increment in dye removal is due to higher crosslinking points and increasing the structure porosity of adsorbent.

Fig. 10D shows the effect of ionic strength of different cations (i.e. Na+, Ca2+ and Al3+) on the dye adsorption capacity of HNC. It was observed that the adsorption efficiency of HNC decreased with increasing concentration of all salt solutions. This can be attributed to the competitive adsorption between the cations and the cationic RhB dye molecules on the surface of HNC adsorbent. Moreover, an additional reason is increasing electrostatic attraction between anionic sites of adsorbent (i.e. sulfunyl and carboxylate groups) and multi-valent cations (i.e. Ca2+ and Al3+), leading to increased ionic crosslinking degree and consequent loss of dye adsorption. Similar observations were observed for adsorption of RhB using gum Karaga grafted with poly(AA) incorporated silicon carbide [80] and adsorption of methylene blue using h-XG/SiO2 hydrogel nanocomposite [81].

Kinetics study of adsorption process for practical applications is one of the most important parameters to evaluate the adsorption efficiency of adsorbent materials. Therefore, we used four well-known kinetic models, i.e. pseudo-first-order (Eq. (3)), pseudo-second-order (Eq. (4)), intra-particle diffusion (Eq. (5)) and Elovich (Eq. (6)) models to fit the dye adsorption experimental data [82]:
(3)
ln
q e
−
q t
= ln
q
e 1
−
k 1
t
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q
e 2
2
+
t
q
e 2
(5)
q t
=
k id
t
1 / 2
+ c
(6)
q t
=
1 β
ln
αβ
+
1 β
ln t
where q
e and q
t are the amounts of dye adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g) and at contact time t (min), respectively. q
e1 and k 1 show the theoretical equilibrium adsorption and rate constant of pseudo-first-order kinetic, respectively. k 2 (gmg−1 min−1) is the pseudo-second-order rate constant and q
e2 is the theoretical adsorbed dye (mg/g). In Eq. (5), k
id (mg/gmin−1/2) is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant. In Eq. (6), α (mg/gmin−1) is the initial sorption rate and β (gmg−1) is the Elovich sorption constant.

All sorption kinetics rate parameters for three initial RhB concentrations (i.e. 40, 80 and 160mgL−1) were calculated and given in Table 2 . According to Table 2, it is revealed that a good agreement of the theoretical adsorption capacity with the experimental data is obtained by using pseudo-second-order kinetic model. For the pseudo-second-order model the correlation coefficients (R2) were varied from 0.9989 to 0.9995, suggesting chemical sorption involving valence forces between adsorbent and adsorbate as the rate-limiting step of the RhB dye adsorption mechanism.

We also analyzed the experimental dye adsorption data according to the four common nonlinear models: Langmuir (Eq. (7)), Freundlich (Eq. (8)), Temkin (Eq. (9)), and Redlich-Peterson (Eq. (10)) in order to describe how dye molecules interact with adsorbents. The Langmuir model assumes that the adsorption process occurs at all specific and equivalent sites of the adsorbent. The Freundlich isotherm assumes neither homogenous sites nor limited levels of adsorption. The Temkin isotherm describes the indirect adsorbent/adsorbate interactions. Finally, the Redlich-Peterson isotherm does not follow ideal monolayer adsorption and can describe the adsorption process over a wide range of concentrations [83].
(7)
C e
q e
=
1
q m
K L
+
C e
q m
(8)
ln
q e
= ln
K F
+
1 n
ln
C e
(9)
q e
= B ln
K T
+ B ln
C e
(10)
q e
=
K RP
C e
1 +
AC e b
where C
e (mgL−1) is the equilibrium concentration of dye solution, q
m (mg/g) is the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, and K
L (Lmg−1) is Langmuir isotherm constant. Freundlich constants, K
F and n, are related to the adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively. Temkin constant B is the related to the heat of adsorption and K
T is the equation binding constant related to the maximum binding energy. K
RP (Lg−1) and A ((Lmg−1)b) are Redlich-Peterson constants; and b is the isotherm exponent indicating the heterogeneity of the adsorbent. All adsorbent parameters of these four models are listed in Table 3 .

The fits of experimental data were evaluated by the non-linear coefficients of determination (R2). The values of R2 for all isotherm models are also presented in Table 3. The R2 values depict that Langmuir isotherm has the best correlation to the experimental data than the other models. Meanwhile, Fig. 11 displays curves comparing the modeled adsorption isotherms with practical data. According to the figure, it can be also confirmed that the Langmuir isotherm best fits with the experimental data for RhB dye adsorption onto HNC adsorbent.

The regeneration of adsorbents is very important from ecological and economic points of views. So, we studied the reusability of the HNC adsorbent by repeating the adsorption-desorption cycle five times at four different temperature values (Fig. 12 ). After the fifth cycle, weak values of RhB dye molecules were liberated from adsorbent. This trend is mainly due to the crosslinking role of CdS-QDs nanoparticles templated within the structure of adsorbent as well as the occurrence of various chelating functional groups in the adsorbent network. Conclusively, the results depict that the prepared HNCs can be used as high-performance recyclable adsorbents for industrial applications.

We have reported herein fabrication of a novel quantum dots (CdS-QDs) incorporated within hydrogel nanocomposites based on CMC polysaccharide. The successful formation of CdS-QDs and structure of the as-prepared HNCs were confirmed by various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. Under the optimized conditions concluded, maximum capacity of swelling in distilled water was found to be 154g/g. The adsorption capacity of RhB dye onto HNCs was also found strongly dependent on adsorbent dose, initial RhB concentration, pH, CdS-QDs content and ionic strength. Moreover, the batch studies illustrated that the kinetics of the dye adsorption process was well fitted with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The adsorption isotherms can be also described well by the Langmuir isotherm model. Finally, the HNC exhibited excellent regeneration capacity at various temperatures. From the obtained results it can be concluded that the synthesized HNCs have the potential to be used as efficient and eco-friendly adsorbent for the removal of different cationic dyes from large volume of industrial wastewater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
J. Polym. Sci. B Polym. Phys.
